course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless the problem needs it.
title: Projections, Components, and Cross Product
sessionGoal: Students should be able to break vectors into parallel/perpendicular parts and use cross products for normals, areas, and planes.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Start with the sentence: projection means shadow.
Do Problem 1 to show parallel and perpendicular pieces.
Do Problem 3 if students need cross product / plane practice.
Emphasize that dot product answers are numbers, but cross product answers are vectors.
sayThisFirst: Projection means the part of one vector that points along another vector.
The perpendicular part is what is left after subtracting the projection.
The cross product gives a vector perpendicular to two 3D vectors.
For planes, the cross product is useful because a plane needs a normal vector.
boardPlan: Write the projection formula and say what each piece means.
Do one 2D projection with a picture.
Use the same idea for distance from a point to a line.
Review the cross product determinant setup.
Use cross product to find a plane through three points.
keyFormulas: a\times b is perpendicular to both a and b.
commonMistakes: Forgetting that projection is a vector, while scalar component is a number.
Projecting onto b when the problem asks to project b onto a.
Forgetting to subtract the projection when finding the perpendicular part.
Cross product order matters: a\times b=-(b\times a).
Using two points to define a plane. You usually need three points or a point plus a normal.
notationWarnings: Use \overrightarrow{AB}=B-A for the vector from A to B.
Write \operatorname{proj}_{a}(b), not just 'projection,' so students know which direction is being used.
For planes, make it clear which vector is the normal.

problems.title: Projection and perpendicular component in R^2
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(a=(2,1)\) and \(b=(3,4)\). Find \(\operatorname{proj}_{a}(b)\), the perpendicular component \(b_{\perp}\), and check that \(b_{\perp}\perp a\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: projection is the shadow of b onto the direction of a. The perpendicular piece is what is left over.

drawInstructions: Draw vector a from the origin.
Draw vector b from the origin.
Draw the projection vector along the same line as a.
Draw the perpendicular piece from the tip of the projection to the tip of b.
Label the right angle between the perpendicular piece and a.

walkthrough.label: Subtract to get the perpendicular piece
Check perpendicular
walkthrough.latex: \[b_{\perp}\cdot a=(-1)(2)+(2)(1)=0.\] Since the dot product is zero, \(b_{\perp}\) is perpendicular to \(a\).

commonMistakes: Using \(b\cdot b\) in the denominator. The denominator is the vector you are projecting onto: \(a\cdot a\).
Stopping after the projection and forgetting the perpendicular component.
Thinking the perpendicular part starts at the origin in the drawing. It is often drawn from the projection tip to the vector tip.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 6 for projections and components.

problems.title: Distance from a point to a line using projection
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(P=(3,2,1)\). Let \(L\) be the line through \(Q=(1,0,2)\) in direction \(d=(2,1,-2)\). Find the distance from \(P\) to \(L\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: the distance from a point to a line is the length of the perpendicular leftover after projecting onto the line direction.

drawInstructions: Draw the line L through Q in direction d.
Draw the perpendicular leftover from the line to P and label it as the distance.

walkthrough.label: Make the vector from the line to the point
Subtract to get the perpendicular vector

commonMistakes: Using \(P\) as if the line went through the origin.
Finding the projected length instead of the perpendicular length.
Forgetting that distance is positive and should be a scalar.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 6 for projections and distance-type questions.

problems.title: Cross product and a plane through three points
problems.studentPromptLatex: Find an equation of the plane through \(A=(1,0,2)\), \(B=(3,1,1)\), and \(C=(0,2,4)\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: three points give two direction vectors in the plane. Their cross product gives a normal vector.

drawInstructions: Draw a tilted plane with points A, B, and C.
Draw \overrightarrow{AB} and \overrightarrow{AC} lying in the plane.
Draw \overrightarrow{AB}\times\overrightarrow{AC} sticking out as the normal vector.
Use point A and the normal vector to write the plane equation.

walkthrough.label: Build two vectors in the plane
Take the cross product to get a normal

commonMistakes: Using \(B-C\) and \(A-C\) is okay, but the point used later must still lie on the plane.
Forgetting that the cross product is the normal, not a vector lying in the plane.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 6 for cross product and plane problems.

wrapUpQuestions: When do you use dot product vs. cross product?
What is the difference between projection and scalar component?
Why does a plane equation need a normal vector?
qualityControlNotes: Cross product checked: AB x AC=(4,-3,5), plane 4x-3y+5z-14=0.
Notation uses arrows for point-to-point vectors.
reviewSummary: Session 14 is ready for Claude rendering with exact visual specs for projection, point-to-line distance, and plane-through-points.
